title: Carbon capture continues to grow, despite challenges | Wood Mackenzie
gist: CCUS keeps growing but is consolidating rather than accelerating, facing political resistance even in Louisiana and needing government support to be viable; Wood Mackenzie's base case sees global capture capacity rising from ~91 Mt/yr today to ~3 Gt/yr by 2060 — still under 6% of global emissions. The column also covers a fragile US-Iran ceasefire and Oracle's Wisconsin data-centre power fight.

insights.claim: Between Q4 2025 and Q1 2026, global CCUS capacity under construction rose 9% and capacity in advanced development rose 23%, while early-stage development capacity fell 7%, suggesting the industry is consolidating rather than growing rapidly.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Falling early-stage pipeline signals a maturation/shakeout phase; near-term capacity growth will come from projects already advanced.

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's base case projects global carbon capture capacity rising from about 91 million tons per year today to about 3 billion tons per year in 2060, which would still capture less than 6% of global emissions 35 years from now.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Even a 33x capacity scale-up leaves CCUS a niche solution, capping its addressable role and the sector's total market.
insights.quote: we project that global carbon capture capacity will rise from about 91 million tons per year today to about 3 billion tons per year in 2060
insights.locator: Even so, Wood Mackenzie still expects strong growth

insights.claim: CCUS usually needs government support to be economically viable, and carbon prices around the world are generally not high enough to justify investment without additional support, with the prices needed to make it take off unlikely to be politically acceptable today.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: CCUS economics are policy-dependent; investment cases hinge on tax credits and carbon-price levels, not market pull.
insights.locator: As things stand, carbon prices around the world

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie LCOE estimates suggest power from gas-fired plants with CCUS would cost more than enhanced geothermal generation but less than new nuclear including small modular reactors.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Positions gas-plus-CCUS in the dispatchable low-carbon merit order: cheaper than nuclear/SMR, dearer than enhanced geothermal.
insights.locator: Carbon capture can also play a role in power generation

insights.claim: For hard-to-abate industrial sectors including cement, steel and fertilisers there are few if any alternatives to CCUS for reducing emissions.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Industrial decarbonisation is CCUS's most defensible demand pocket where it faces little substitution risk.

insights.claim: Public and political resistance to CCUS even in a US oil-and-gas heartland like Louisiana is a symptom of the industry's challenges, exemplified by Senate primary contender John Fleming making opposition to carbon storage a campaign plank before losing a runoff.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Local property-rights and NIMBY opposition is a durable permitting risk for CO2 storage projects, even in friendly jurisdictions.
insights.quote: deprive the property rights of Louisiana citizens for the benefit of wealthy corporations seeking to store dangerous carbon dioxide underground
insights.locator: Carbon capture has become a hot political issue

insights.claim: CCUS has enjoyed bipartisan US support, with tax credits expanded and increased by a Democratic Congress in 2022 and retained and increased again by Republicans in the One Big Beautiful Bill.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Bipartisan tax-credit continuity gives CCUS policy stability across administrations, a key underwriting input.
insights.locator: Because it is a way to reconcile continued fossil fuel use

insights.claim: Big tech has been cautious about investing directly in CCUS, with the flagship project being Broadwing Energy's new gas-fired power plant with carbon capture in Illinois, backed by Google.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Direct tech capital into CCUS generation remains thin; the sector can't yet count on hyperscaler balance sheets for capacity.
insights.locator: The big tech companies, which need always-available

insights.claim: Tech support for carbon capture has mostly come via credit purchases: Stripe, Google, Salesforce and Anthropic committed US$915 million of carbon credits through Frontier (after a US$1 billion 2022 commitment), while Microsoft dominated the market with more than 90% of worldwide purchases last year.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Microsoft's >90% share exposes acute buyer-concentration risk in carbon removal demand; the market rests on one buyer.
insights.quote: Microsoft has been the dominant player in the carbon credit market, accounting for more than 90% of purchases worldwide last year.
insights.locator: Tech companies' support for carbon capture

insights.claim: CCUS, like geothermal and new nuclear, is not a first choice for tech companies driving US generation investment because speed to power is critical and the extra build time for a CCUS system can be a deal-breaker.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Time-to-power, not just cost, disadvantages CCUS for data-centre power procurement; it competes on a later timeline.
insights.locator: CCUS, like geothermal energy and new nuclear

insights.claim: The US-Iran ceasefire remains formally in place despite renewed strikes, with Iran attacking a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, US air strikes in response, and Iranian drones and missiles launched at Bahrain and Kuwait.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A fragile ceasefire keeps Hormuz tail risk live for oil and shipping despite calm prices.
insights.locator: US-Iran ceasefire formally remains in place

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's Vesseltracker counted about 50 vessels a day passing through the Strait of Hormuz over the past few days, versus 10 to 20 a day for most of March, April and May, showing traffic materially recovered despite renewed clashes.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Vessel-count data gives a real-time gauge of Hormuz normalization; still below pre-war throughput.
insights.quote: about 50 vessels a day passing through the strait over the past few days, compared to 10 to 20 a day for most of March, April and May

insights.claim: Oil markets stayed calm despite renewed US-Iran tensions, with Brent crude trading at about US$73 a barrel on Tuesday morning.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Muted price response signals the market has largely de-risked the Hormuz conflict for now.
insights.locator: Oil markets remained calm despite the latest tensions

insights.claim: Oracle is suing Wisconsin regulators over large-load tariff rules requiring financial guarantees from data-centre customers, arguing the requirements impose substantial, unreasonable costs wholly disproportionate to Wisconsin Electric's risk.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A test case for who bears grid-investment risk of data-centre load; outcome shapes ratepayer-protection tariffs nationally.
insights.locator: Oracle is suing regulators in Wisconsin

insights.claim: Oracle is developing a nearly 1-gigawatt data centre campus on a 672-acre Wisconsin site with Vantage Data Centers and OpenAI, a US$15 billion project that is one of the largest private-sector investments in the state's history.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Illustrates the gigawatt-scale, multi-billion-dollar power demand driving utility and tariff conflicts.
insights.locator: Oracle is developing a nearly 1-gigawatt data centre campus

insights.claim: President Trump publicly urged gasoline retailers to cut prices immediately toward about US$2.50 a gallon versus a current US average of about US$3.85, after crude fell to about US$68 a barrel following the US-Iran ceasefire.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Political pressure on fuel retail margins is a headline risk for downstream marketers if prices stay elevated.
insights.quote: Gasoline Retailers must get their Prices down, IMMEDIATELY!

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's latest US Energy Storage Monitor with the American Clean Power Association shows rising utility-scale storage installations with forecasts indicating more growth into the 2030s.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Utility-scale storage growth remains a structural tailwind for the battery/BESS value chain.
